coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein (BRCA1) using poly (dopamine-beta cyclodextrine-Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide) doped with silver nanoparticles: A new platform in early stage diagnosis of breast cancer and efficient management
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Feyziazar, Mahsa
Solhi, Elham
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
Soleymani, Jafar
Shadjou, Nasrin
Jouyban, Abolghasem
Mahboob, Soltanali
coredata.dc:description: Breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein (BRCA1) is antioncogenes in women, who are genetically predisposed to breast cancer. Therefore, development of a reliable method for sensitive and selective detection of BRCA1 with low concentration level is very important. In this research work a novel immunoassay based on signal amplification strategy of multiple polymers to recognition of BRCA1 protein was developed. The engineered immunoassay prepared by poly (dopamine-beta cyclodextrine-Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide doped by silver nanoparticles (P[DA-β-CD/CTAB])-AgNPs) and functionalized mesoporous silica (MCM-41-SO3H) composite fabricated on the glassy carbon electrode with a large surface area which was prepared a new platform to immobilization of primary antibodies and provide excellent conductivity. To further amplify the electrochemical signal, MCM-41-SO3H provides suitable pores volume and functional groups to capture more horseradish peroxidase-labeled antibodies (HPR-Ab2) and enhances the conductivity. The proposed immunoassay displays suitable analytical performance for the monitoring of BRCA1 with a linear range from 0.01565–10 pg/mL (by DPV) and 0.625–20 pg/mL (by SWV) and a low limit of quantification of 0.003 pg/mL. Also, satisfactory results are obtained for the determination of BRCA1 in human plasma samples and MCF-7 cancer cell lysates, indicating the potential of the immunoassay to be applied in clinical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomedical analysis
Tumor protein BRCA1
Immunoassay
Breast cancer
Immunocomplex
Polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of mobile crystalline material-41 grafted by sulfonic acid (MCM-41-SO3H)
Selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the engineered immunosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Institutes for Medical Research Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number [ 977069 ] from the National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. Also, we thank Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DPV (A), and SWV (B) obtained for bare GCE, P(DA-β-CD), P (CTAB)-GCE (B), P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB], P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs, Ab1/P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, BRCA1/Ab1/P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, and HABMS/BRCA1/Ab1/P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE respectively in 0.01 mM riboflavin. Step potential = 0.001, Amplitude time = 0.02 s, Frequency = 10 Hz, Scan Rate = 0.05 V s−1.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein (BRCA1) using poly (dopamine-beta cyclodextrine-Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide) doped with silver nanoparticles: A new platform in early stage diagnosis of breast cancer and efficient management

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Higher Education Institute of Rab-Rashid, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nanotechnology Research Center, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein (BRCA1) is antioncogenes in women, who are genetically predisposed to breast cancer. Therefore, development of a reliable method for sensitive and selective detection of BRCA1 with low concentration level is very important. In this research work a novel immunoassay based on signal amplification strategy of multiple polymers to recognition of BRCA1 protein was developed. The engineered immunoassay prepared by poly (dopamine-beta cyclodextrine-Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide doped by silver nanoparticles (P[DA-β-CD/CTAB])-AgNPs) and functionalized mesoporous silica (MCM-41-SO3H) composite fabricated on the glassy carbon electrode with a large surface area which was prepared a new platform to immobilization of primary antibodies and provide excellent conductivity. To further amplify the electrochemical signal, MCM-41-SO3H provides suitable pores volume and functional groups to capture more horseradish peroxidase-labeled antibodies (HPR-Ab2) and enhances the conductivity. The proposed immunoassay displays suitable analytical performance for the monitoring of BRCA1 with a linear range from 0.01565–10 pg/mL (by DPV) and 0.625–20 pg/mL (by SWV) and a low limit of quantification of 0.003 pg/mL. Also, satisfactory results are obtained for the determination of BRCA1 in human plasma samples and MCF-7 cancer cell lysates, indicating the potential of the immunoassay to be applied in clinical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer type 1 susceptibility protein (BRCA1) is antioncogenes in women, who are genetically predisposed to breast cancer. It is normally expressed in the cells of breast and other tissues, where they are involved in the repair of chromosomal damage with a key role in the error-free repair of DNA double helix breaks. Malfunction of BRCA1, due to mutation of the corresponding gene, increases the risk of breast cancer [1–4].

Providing of a trustworthy method for sensitive and selective detection of BRCA1 with low concentration level is very important. In the past decades, a large number of techniques for BRCA1 determination have been reported, including chemiluminescence immunoassay, quartz crystal microbalance, enzyme-linked immunosorption assay, optical immunoassay. However, all these traditional immunoassay require complicated operations, expensive instruments, and long analysis time. Hence, the development of practical methods for the detection of BRCA1 is important from health viewpoints. The attractive properties of electrochemical devices are extremely promising for improving the efficiency of cancer diagnostics and therapy monitoring. With further development and resources, such portable devices are expected to speed up the diagnosis of cancer, making analytical results available at patient bedside or physician office within few minutes. Electrochemical methods are fast, low-cost and easy-to-use because electrochemical instrumentation is usually compact, reliable, sensitive and relatively inexpensive. Compared with other electrochemical sensors, the sandwich-type electrochemical biosensor is more selective and sensitive, and more suitable for detection of tumor markers in low concentrations [5,6].

Signal amplification has been successfully performed by advanced nanomaterials, recently. Nano-materials suggest some outstanding prospects for interfacing biological recognition events with transduction of electronic signals and for designing new bioelectronic devices exhibiting with novel functions [7,8]. Since the discovery of the conducting polymers in 1977s by Shirowska et al. [9], many scientists have been working on finding the applications for the newly introduced polymers and creating a pathway in the sensing world [10]. Thanks to their unique characters, including biocompatibility, fascinating electrocatalytic activity, large surface area, excellent conductivity, and stability, which is coupled with their ease of processing/modification and rich chemistry; polymer based electrochemical sensors were noticed as a promising platform for the sensitive detection of biological compounds [11–14]. The goal of the covalent attachment of biomolecules to the surface of polymer could be achieved by using polymer derivatives (e.g. carboxylated or aminated), which provides plentiful of functional groups for long term retention and stabilization of biomolecules [15-38]. Considering the electrochemical methods, electrochemical polymerization not only provide a simple and reproducible formation of polymer, but also permits easy control over the properties of the polymeric coating such as morphology and thickness, compare to other robust methods (e.g. chemicals) [15-18].

Therefore, we developed a simple and environment-friendly approach for the preparation of P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB] doped by Ag NPs hybrid materials. For the first time, a novel sandwich-type immunoassay to recognition of BRCA1 based on P(CTAB), PDA-β-CD, MSNP-SO3H and AgNPs was developed in this work. In our new design, the (P[DA-β-CD/CTAB]-Ag NPs-GCE) was utilized to captured primary antibodies (Ab1) to enhanced electron transfer rate. MSNP-SO3H was applied to immobilize the secondary antibody (HPR-Ab2) to realize additional signal amplification. The electrochemical response of the fabricated immunosensor was dramatically enhanced. By square wave voltammetry (SWV), the assay showed ultrasensitive response to BRCA1 in unprocessed human plasma samples and MCF-7 cancer cell lysates. Our preliminary study indicates that as-proposed immunosensor could be used to recognition BRCA1 in clinical application.

Human BRCA1 ELISA kit (CanAg /BRCA1 EIA) including BRCA1 antigen, anti-BRCA1 standard, biotinilated solution and avidin-biotin-peroxidase complex (ABC), standard buffer and dilute solutions were obtained from CanAg Diagonestic AB Technology Co., (Gotenberg, Sweden). Disodium hydrogen orthophosphate, potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate potassium chloride, sodium nitrite, silver Nnitrate (AgNO3), hydrochloric acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (0.05 M) was prepared by Na2HPO4 (0.1 M) and NaH2PO4 (0.1 M) with 0.15 M of NaCl in deionized water. Human plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran).

In SWV investigations, the scan rate was adjusted for 0.05 V s−1, and in SWV, the modulation amplitude was 50 mV, the step potential was 6 mV, and the apparent scan rate was 15 mV s−1, in the potential range of −0.2 to 0.1 V. All measurements were the average of at least three replicates. Riboflavin (6,7-dimethyl-9-(d-1-ribityl)-isoalloxazine), is a water-soluble vitamin belonging to the B-complex group. Riboflavin (RF), is an essential precursor of two coenzymes: flavin mononucleotide and flavin adenosine dinucleotide. The electrochemical behavior of these coenzymes has been studied [31]. These compounds by accepting and donating two electrons in the isoalloxazine ring, show reversible two-electron reduction and oxidation waves both of which are due to over-lapping one-electron waves [31]. Electron transfer reaction upon reduction is as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mobile crystalline material-41 grafted by sulfonic acid (MCM-41-SO3H)
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of MCM-41 was carried out according to the procedure reported by [32]. The obtained MCM-41 was calcinated at 540 °C for 6 h to remove the cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) which is used as a templating agent. The SO3H moieties anchored to the surface of the MCM-41 using the strategy which was reported in our previous work [33]. The morphology of the synthesize MCM 41-SO3H was approved and characterized according to our previous work [33].

Electrochemical measurements were recorded using a computer-controlled electrochemical system comprising of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) using NOVA 1.11 software. The conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell was set using GCE, Ag/AgCl and a platinum wire as the working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. The surface morphology of the modified electrodes and the nanoparticles was evaluated with a FESEM (field emission scanning electron microscopy) analysis which was conducted on a TESCAN system of FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). The elemental analysis of the electrode surface was recorded by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic) model.

Surface morphology of the bare and modified surfaces was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. S2(A–E (See supporting Information)) represents the scanning electron micrographs of bare GCE (A), P(DA-β-CD), P (CTAB)-GCE (B), P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB] (C), P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs (D), Ab1/P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, and BRCA1/Ab1/P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE, HABMS/BRCA1/Ab1/P(CTAB-CS)-Ag NPs-GCE respectively. Bare GCE has a rough surface due to irregular graphite sheets in its framework. Fig. S2B (See supporting Information) clearly shows that the morphology of the GCE changed after the electrodeposition of P(DA-β-CD) by featuring a three-dimensional network with rod-like structure. CTAB were embedded in the polymer film providing a network structure which results in increased surface area and improved electrical conductivity. Similar results was obtained for P (CTAB)-GCE (B), P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]. Grafting of P(DA-β-CD) by CTAB during the electropolymerization involved polymer film growth on GCE. For the P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs composite, a totally different morphology was obtained indicating the formation of a nanocomposite film. The coated layer provides a high surface area, which leads to enhance the antibody loading. A different morphology was developed over the electrode surface in the presence of antibody. The increased surface roughness implies antibody probe binding to the polymer. Finally after immobilization of Ab and BRCA1 antigen, morphology of the electrode was changed which confirmed successfully preparation of immunocomplex between antibody and antigen.

Stepwise construction process of the electrochemical immunosensor was investigated by DPV, and SWV in 0.01 mM riboflavin as shown in Fig. 1 . The characteristic peak of bare GCE (curve a) was markedly increased after its modification with P(DA-β-CD). Interestingly, the data obtained results clearly show that the combination of P(DA-β-CD) and CTAB definitely alter the nature of SWV oxidation peak current (low oxidation potential and high peak current). That might be related to the excellent property of P(DA)-ERGO, such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. As can be obtained from SWV curves, the oxidation peak current of P(DA-β-CD)-GCE increased in accordance with the increase of the effective surface area after modifying CTAB on the electrode proportional to bare GCE. Likewise, after surface modification of the P(DA-β-CD) by CTAB, the oxidation peak current significantly increased, which shows that the desirable changes have been successfully applied on the surface of P(DA-β-CD)-GCE. Presence of a conductive CTAB layer on the P(DA-β-CD)-GCE surface with a large surface area shows the signal amplification for the redox probe, due to the improvement of the electron transfer process. As it is obvious, changes in the current responses are completely distinguishable from SWVs, which highlights the SWV's capability in tracing small changes on this conductive platform in the presence of other reagents compare to the represented voltammograms in the same condition. To make a more stable and promising immunosensing platform further modification requires to create a more binding sites to enhance sensitivity. Metal NPs provides a high surface area and abundant functional groups which improve their dispersivity, stability and sensitivity. Thus, AgNPs were found more suitable for immunosensor application. Thus, it provides a favorable condition for immobilization of the antibody against the desired analyte like cancer biomarker. Electrodeposition of the Ag NPs on the surface of P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB] led to increase in the peak current because of high electron transfer effect.

In contrast, when biotinylated BRCA1 antibodies (Ab1) were assembled on the porous 3D network modified electrode by the EDC/NHS reaction, the peak currents of the P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-AgNPs-GCE decreased obviously, indicating antibodies (Ab1) were successfully immobilized and retarded the electron transfer due to their poor conductivity. In fact, the antibodies act as an insulter and reduce the electron transfer rate. After blocking the nonspecific binding sites by BSA, the peak current further decreased. This could be attributed to the blocking effect of BSA on the electron transfer process. Following that, the peak currents further decreased after incubation with BRCA1. The reason was that the formation of the antigen-antibody complexes on the surface of the electrode formed poor conductive layers and limited the diffusion of riboflavin. Upon the dropping of HABMS labels onto the modified electrode, the peak currents decreased again, indicating successful capture of HABMS.
It is important to point out that, the results of DPV and SWV are similar and confirmed each other.

The electroactive surface area (A) values, that are different from the geometric one of different electrodes, are measured by peak current versus scan rate (Fig. S3A–D (see supporting information)). The Values of different electrodes are calculated by CVs using the Equation Γ =
Q nFA
. In the above equation, Γ is the surface coverage of the electrode reaction substance, Q is the charge involved in the reaction, which is calculated from the reduction peak area of the voltammogram by integrating the reduction current against time (Q = ∫ i dt). n is the number of electron transferred reaction, F is Faraday constant, and A is the electrode surface area. The A value of P(DA-β-CD)-GCE and P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB] are 0.98 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 and 2.15 × 10−6 cm2 s−1, while P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs has a higher active surface area with a value of 9.12 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 after final modification. Therefore, the diffusion layer thickness of P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs leads to higher peak currents due to an increase in the particle size and active surface area with an A value of 9.12 × 10−6 cm2 s−1. Besides, as the microscopic area increases due to the presence of PDA, the available catalytic active sites increase.

It can be proposed that the long term stability is related to the high chemical and mechanical stability of the P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs-GCE nanocomposites and covalent attachment of antibody functionalized AgNPs on the surface of the modified electrode; therefore, this nanocomposite indicates promising properties as a useful platform for immobilization of antibody and preparation of immunosensor. For confirmation, cyclic stability of P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs were investigated by CV techniques. As can be seen, no change was observed on the redox peak currents/potential to 5 cycle which confirm suitable stability of the organic-inorganic platform in aqueous solution (Fig. S4 (See supporting information)).

The electrochemical behavior of the BRCA1 immunosensor was further investigated by DPV and SWV techniques which have a better sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio compared with CV. Fig. S5 (A&C) (see supporting information) displays the DPV and SWV responses of the immunosensor for different BRCA1 concentrations under optimal conditions in 0.01 M Riboflavin. Fig. S5 (B&D) (see supporting information) shows the corresponding calibration curve. As shown in the DPV results, well-defined peaks were clearly generated in the range from 0.01565–10 pg/mL (by DPV) and 0.625–20 pg/mL (by SWV), which were proportional to the concentrations of BRCA1. The low limit of quantification (LLOQ) is calculated to be about 0.003 pg/mL. Compared with other biosensors reported by analytical studies (Table 2), the developed immunosensor showed better or at least comparable analytical performance in view of the linear range and the limit of detection. Such excellent sensing performance can be attributed following factors of: (a) excellent conductivity and unique structural features P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs; (b) large active surface area of P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs modified electrode provides high density and a favorable microenvironment for oriented antibody immobilization retaining the biological activity; (3) To further enhance the electrochemical signal, HBAMS was used as a tracer for good catalytic reduction of riboflavin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the engineered immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical evaluations were performed in the presence of some coexistent proteins as interferences to evaluate the immunosensor selectivity. For this purpose, DPV and SWV experiments were carried out to check the cross-reactivity of the BRCA1 with possible coexisting and interfering compounds, such as BSA, CA 15-3, CA 125, PSA, and mixture. The prepared immunosensor was incubated with interfering compounds in the supporting electrolyte. The peak current produced by the prepared immunosensor is almost similar (Fig. S6 (see supporting information)). The successive addition of higher concentrations of interfering compounds did not make any appreciable increment in peak current, shows that the engineered sensor exhibits remarkable selectivity towards BRCA1. This can be attributed to the suitable specificity of the proposed immunosensor to detection of BRCA1.

The stability of the electrode in the terms of peak current/potential was also studied by storing the electrode at 4 °C for 24 h. Stability of the prepared immunosensor was tested by measuring the decrease in voltammetric current during repetitive DPV measurements (Fig. S7 (see supporting information)). Obtained results show that this immunosensor has remarkable stability after 24 h. When the electrode was stored in the 4 °C, the current response remained almost unchanged for about one day. It was found that the peak current of the immunosensor is not stable in various days and the signal was not retained while the peak potentials were not change (See Scheme 1 ).

To check the inter-electrode reproducibility of the modified electrode, four modified electrodes were tested simultaneously by recording DPVs in in 0.01 M Riboflavin, containing was 6.21%. Therefore, the proposed immunosensor imparts a suitable stability onto measurements of BRCA1 by DPV.

To examine the potential practical applications of the immunosensor, different concentrations of BRCA1 were spiked into human plasma samples (Fig. S8 (see supporting information)). The human plasma samples were not processed. Then, the calibration curved was obtained. The recovery rates of the four concentrations in plasma samples were observed in the range from 89.2% to 97.5% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) (n = 2) was less than 3.98%, suggesting the acceptable reliability and accuracy of the fabricated immunosensor.

In addition, SWV techniques were used for the analysis of BRCA1 in the breast cancer cell MCF-7 culture media. To this end, using the prepared immunosensor and standard addition method, we analyzed for the detection of the BRCA1 in the cancer cell lysates such as breast cancer cell MCF-7. The proposed sandwich type immunosensor could detect the BRCA1 in these samples. Fig. S9 (see supporting information) shows the SWVs of BRCA1 in the cancer cell lines cultured during three days. Under the optimized experimental conditions, we investigated the performance of the developed electrochemical immunosensor in quantitative analysis of cancer cell lysates. The peak currents of the proposed immunosensor were linearly proportional to the MCF-7 cells concentration. The sensitivity of the developed immunosensor was investigated by varying the concentration of MCF-7 cells lysates with the sensitive DPV detection. As shown in Fig. 10 the oxidation peak current decreased gradually with the increase in the MCF-7 cell lysates concentration. The average oxidation peak current had a good linear relationship versus the concentration of MCF-7 cell in the range from 100 to 700 cells/mL−1 with a LLOQ of 100 cell/mL, which was comparable to those reported by most of assays and other electrochemical and fluorescence cytosensing strategy for cancer cell detection. The linear regression equation was calculated as I (μA) = −0.0018C MCF-7+ 3.4979, R2 = 0.8881.

In summary, an effective and ultrasensitive sandwich-type immunosensor was successfully fabricated to detect BRCA1 in human plasma samples. Functionalized mesoporous silica was used as the signal amplifying probe to fix HRP-anti-BRCA1 Ab2 and unique P[(DA-β-CD)/CTAB]-Ag NPs as the supporting substrate after immobilization of biotinylated antibody. Under the optimized operating conditions, the prepared immunosensor presents a suitable linear range from 0.01565–10 pg/mL (by DPV) and 0.625–20 pg/mL (by SWV) with a low limit of quantification (LLOQ) of 0.003 pg/mL. The good performance might be attributed to the enhanced surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, fast electron transfer, good catalysis properties and desirable biocompatibility of the immunosensor. All these features demonstrate that the proposed immunosensor can be used as a versatile platform for other biomarkers and has application potential in biological and clinical diagnoses.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number [977069] from the National Institutes for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. Also, we thank Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2018.11.029.
